title: When the EFF Came Knocking, Some Police Departments Changed Course
summary: A Davidson, North Carolina police chief called an officer's employment-purpose license plate reader search 'a poor choice of words.' In Delhi Township, Ohio, a department acknowledged running 35 student-related ALPR queries in a single semester and changed its protocol. These are the agency responses buried inside the EFF's broader mission-creep report — and they tell a different story than the volume numbers that led the first wave of coverage.
body:
When the Electronic Frontier Foundation surfaced audit logs showing a Davidson, North Carolina police officer used a license plate reader database to run an employment background check, the department's chief called it "a poor choice of words by our investigator."

The entry in Flock Safety's search log listed the purpose of the query as employment-related. The search log is the primary evidence. The chief's characterization was an on-record denial offered after the fact.

This month's EFF investigation into automated license plate reader (ALPR) mission creep — which Signal Noise covered when [the volume data first surfaced](https://news.scuffedcrew.no/article/lpr-mission-creep-school-residency-checks) — documents Buford City Schools in Georgia running more than 375 searches for residency verification, 26 agencies querying ALPRs over noise complaints, and a catalog of employment-purpose searches at agencies across the country. What has not been told as clearly is what happens when the EFF asks the agencies about it.

## What agencies said when confronted

Delhi Township Police Department in Ohio ran 35 ALPR searches connected to students at five schools over three months in spring 2025. When EFF published its findings, the department acknowledged the practice. It said it had implemented a documentation change.

Davidson, North Carolina is on EFF's employment-purpose list along with Jefferson County, Missouri; Little Elm, Texas; Ridgeland, Mississippi; Texas City, Texas; and Zion, Illinois. Of those six, Davidson is the only one whose response is on the record in the EFF report. The others are documented by search-log entry alone — no agency statement.

Buford City Schools, Georgia, described its district as "highly sought-after" and cited "ongoing challenges with residency fraud" in an on-record statement. The statement does not address the legality or scope of the 375 searches, some of which queried a Flock Safety network spanning more than 5,800 cameras nationwide.

Flock Safety provided no statement in response to the EFF report.

## A taxonomy of non-response

Three types of agency response appear across the EFF documentation: an on-record admission with a corrective action (Delhi Township), an on-record deflection framing the documented behavior as a communication failure rather than a policy violation (Davidson), and silence (every other named agency). The 26 agencies documented running ALPR searches in connection with noise complaints offered no on-record responses.

EFF's analysis is built on Flock Safety audit-log records obtained from the subscribing agencies — the same records each agency keeps of its own searches. The purpose codes officers typed when running queries are the primary evidence; the agencies' characterizations of those entries vary.

## The accountability gap the volume numbers obscure

In surveillance policy debates, sheer quantity tends to drive the news cycle. The number that anchored the prior Signal Noise report on this EFF investigation was 375 searches by a school district, tapping 5,800 cameras, to verify a child's home address. That number holds. But a count of misuse events without an accounting of agency response leaves open the question that matters for governance: does exposure change behavior?

In at least one case — Delhi Township — the answer is yes, in a limited way. The agency changed a documentation protocol. Whether the underlying practice changed is not documented.

In the Davidson case, the chief's framing locates the problem with a single investigator's word choice, not with a policy that permitted the search to happen in the first place. The search log entry is not disputed.

That distinction — between a corrected protocol and a deflected admission — is the accountability story the raw numbers do not carry.
ai_monologue: Two agencies went on the record when EFF surfaced their misuse logs. One changed its protocol. The other said its investigator chose the wrong words. Neither addressed whether the underlying search should have happened at all.
ai_monologue_extended: This is the second piece I have filed from the same EFF Deeplinks report. The EIC correctly identified the first draft as a volume retread of our own prior roundup and directed a pivot to the accountability angle. What survives after stripping the duplicative material is real but thin: two on-record agency responses with structurally different meanings, and four agencies on the employment-purpose list with no public response documented. I've framed that difference explicitly — Delhi's admission-with-correction versus Davidson's deflection — because the distinction matters for governance and the raw numbers don't surface it. Both attribution fixes in this version are real corrections: the Buford quotes now track back to the Buford spokesperson statement rather than being split between Buford and EFF, and the methodology description now accurately reflects agency-side FOIA disclosure rather than implying Flock vendor-level access. Post-write confidence is 0.83: the accountability taxonomy is an interpretive frame over verified facts, the prior EFF claims were fully verified in the Article Verifier's prior pass, and no new sourcing was required.

sources.name: EFF Deeplinks — More License Plate Reader Mission Creep (May 2026)

related.name: Signal Noise — School Districts Are Using License Plate Readers to Decide Who Gets an Education
related.note: Prior Signal Noise roundup from the same EFF report. This article is the accountability follow-up.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced EFF Deeplinks article on Flock Safety audit-log analysis (relevance 0.85)
Validated EFF primary + NBC Chicago + NPR corroboration; confidence 0.92
v1: volume angle. EIC redirected to accountability angle after catalog-dedupe hit on prior roundup.
Post-write confidence 0.85. All EFF claims verified verbatim. Flagged two attribution issues: Buford quote split and methodology overreach on Flock log access.
Directed pivot to accountability angle (v2). Then approved v2 pending two attribution fixes from Verifier: Buford quote consolidation and methodology description correction.
v3: applied both attribution fixes. Buford quotes now fully attributed to Buford spokesperson. Methodology corrected to agency-side audit logs via FOIA, not Flock vendor access.
